data.title: State Street: An Undervalued Opportunity, Betting On Continued Organic Growth And Market Recovery
data.description:
It's time for another 1-year anniversary article, and this time I picked State Street Corporation (STT) to follow up on since my last article in April 2025, when I called this stock a buy.

In fact, since my very first coverage in May 2023, when I called it a buy, it is up around +80%, putting it among my best-performing buy ratings, and is up since my subsequent bullish ratings, showing that the market shared my high confidence in this financial firm.

What I've always loved about STT is the diversified business model, strong position in terms of regulatory capital, and solid credit ratings.

For readers just tuning in to my coverage of this stock, if it has been off your radar, consider from its profile that it not only does some traditional banking for institutional clients but also capitalizes on so much data via its State Street Alpha platform.

With its next earnings results in a few weeks in mid-April, now is an opportune time to dive into this stock again and see if it can potentially achieve "alpha" for investors.

Thesis Summary

After my updated research, I reaffirmed a buy rating on this stock yet again.

Today's article will show that despite the current global market environment, there still remain positive forecasts for markets in 2026, and State Street showed organic growth drivers, an even more improved balance sheet profile this time, a compelling dividend growth case, and an undervaluation opportunity in relation to upside potential.

The worksheet below shows how I rated each of 8 rating categories this time and how they contributed to the overall average score.

Read on to find out how each section contributed to my overall view.

Macro and Sector Trends

When it comes to macro and sector trends, I gave this section a buy score after considering some forecasts as well as 2025 performance in the face of various market shocks.

In considering the specific type of bank this is, which is far from being consumer-oriented or regional but rather a global firm that manages several funds, many of which are featured in Morningstar, but also has solutions for markets and ways to turn data into actionable insights.

So, I think a major impact on this type of firm could be rising or falling equity markets, as that is related to investor confidence and trading volumes and fees earned on managing assets.

While my earlier articles this year cited some earlier, more rosy forecasts for 2026 markets before the current geopolitical situation in the Mideast and subsequent oil shock, it makes sense why some would be more cautious now, but perhaps not quite justifying a sky-is-falling view.

For instance, on March 26th, equity researchers at Barclays "expect aggregate earnings per share for the S&P 500 to rise 16% this year, which would be the strongest year of growth since the post-pandemic rebound."

If this forecast materializes, it could certainly drive investor confidence, despite the current headwinds. STT, as a US-based stock, could benefit. Interestingly, the same Barclays analysis also favors US stocks, saying their analysts "remain constructive on risk, leaning into US equities."

Q1 is almost done, and so market watchers will certainly be paying close attention to earnings season coming up soon, and I think a few major earnings beats among big names in financials could give financial stocks a needed boost.

Consider that just days ago, another financial site reported that Goldman Sachs (GS) continued to have a positive view of earnings potential this year:

The fundamental baseline for U.S. corporations remains solid despite the macro headwinds. Goldman Sachs maintains a base-case forecast of 12% EPS growth for the S&P 500 in 2026.

The upcoming earnings season will be a critical litmus test.

Additionally, the market is no stranger to shocks, volatility, and political announcements that seem to change weekly and move the needle since they make huge headlines.

Last year, for instance, we saw what amounted to potential tariff shocks, yet the market still came out of that shock ahead. For instance, State Street's own analysis published this month (Investor Resilience, pg. 17) pointed to a market that proved resilient in the face of shocks:

Despite heightened uncertainty and market volatility during 2025-driven by developments in international trade, geopolitics and Artificial Intelligence-institutional investors maintained a supportive risk stance for much of the year.

Therefore, I am still modestly bullish when it comes to macro and sector trends.

Competitive Positioning

Moving on, I considered the positioning of STT vs. similar peers, and here I gave it a hold score.

For peer comparables, I picked the following, which span across the realm of custody banking and asset managers: Bank of New York Mellon (BK), Northern Trust (NTRS), and Invesco (IVZ).

I considered some historic performance data in this peer group, and State Street fell behind when it came to top-line growth, with a 5-year revenue CAGR of 3.6%, putting it 3rd in this group, and it was also in that position for return on equity, with an ROE of 11%. In both cases, its peer Northern Trust dominated those metrics.

Since I too try to achieve alpha in my own portfolio, readers may want to know if STT was able to beat a major benchmark like the S&P 500 and achieve alpha, at least not in relation to that index. Momentum data shows that although STT had a positive price return (+107%) over the last decade, it was easily beaten by its close peer BK and did not come close to beating the benchmark, indicating some weaker momentum from investors perhaps.

While the above metrics may paint STT as a relatively muted company vs. peers like this, on a qualitative basis, let's not ignore that it also has been very competitive in the software/data niche, going beyond traditional banking. For instance, in the FY25 Q4 earnings deck (pg. 7), within its "software and processing fees" section, it mentioned "4Q25 ARR (annualized recurring revenue) increased ~11% YoY driven by continued SaaS client conversions and implementations."

Thus, based on both quantitative/historic performance metrics and also qualitative factors, I gave this stock a neutral view in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Now we are at the point of looking at the income statement, and the top-line growth factors I considered boosted my view towards a strong buy score for this category.

We already mentioned growth in ARR within the firm's software segment, but it is worth considering organic growth in terms of new users, since that could drive future subscriber revenue if those become sticky users, and we see from Q4 results (pg. 7) there has been steady growth in new bookings:

While State Street's intense focus on data and tech is impressive, the firm also boasted organic growth in its investment management niche, which matters because this fees-driven segment can grow in two ways, organic inflows and market appreciation of asset values, and organic growth is, to me, more indicative of attracting client monies to the firm.

For instance, Q4 results (pg. 5) show that Q4 saw net inflows across all asset classes in terms of AUM:

Growth can also come from the acquisition of other businesses, something I often follow, and notable about State Street was the acquisition last November of PriceStats. The future-looking view here is that it could start enhancing State Street's data intelligence offering in 2026 and beyond, since PriceStats was known for "offering insights into inflation trends and purchasing power that complement traditional government indicators."

Seeking Alpha data on forward revenue growth for STT is positive, with forward revenue growth at +5.6%, further complementing the growth thesis.

This comes on the heels of 2 straight years of revenue growth for STT, as income statement data shows, going from $11.8B in FY23 to $13.8B in FY25.

So, I think STT deserves a highly bullish score in this section.

Earnings Outlook

Moving down the income statement to earnings, after considering some factors impacting earnings, I gave the stock a buy rating, and here is why.

As I mentioned in my recent article on another bank, Barclays (BCS), I learned at the recent Bloomberg Adria regional conference called Investment Outlook 2026 that surgical selection of stocks rather than a wide diversification net was the overwhelming sentiment, but also that equities are still the asset class most in favor over others.

With that said, even though State Street saw a YoY growth in net income for FY25, I surgically took a closer look at margins, and I think the operating margin best shows the core business efficiency, which in this case puts Invesco well ahead of the pack, with State Street just behind.

In terms of net profit margin, we can see that STT is well below some of the peaks it saw several years ago:

A key potential driver of high costs at this type of business is ironically tied to what I said makes it competitive, and that is its positioning as a provider of market data/intelligence/software solutions. Consider that from the Q4 earnings release (pg. 3) we see that in Q4 expenses, "information systems and communications increased 16%."

I expect this to continue, as being a data-driven firm seems to be the direction they will continue going. In fact, Chairman and CEO Ron O'Hanley said in his Q4 results comments (pg. 1) that "as we enter 2026, we will execute against our strategic priorities, including further embedding technology and artificial intelligence to drive further transformation across the franchise."

So, in the upcoming earnings call in mid-April, I expect more of the same and further language on how the firm continues to invest in its tech stack to support the strategy.

The consensus is confident about near-term earnings, giving this firm +13 upward EPS revisions vs. just 2 downward, and the consensus expects 11-14% YoY EPS growth this year and next, which I think will attract bulls to either hold or buy more of this stock, despite the current macro environment, if they are to think in terms of surgical selectivity.

Thus, in my selectivity, although operating margins could use some improvement, I also understand it should be taken into context with the amount of tech investment this firm has been doing and will continue to do, so I am bullish in this section.

Balance Sheet Risk

Moving on to another fundamental financial statement, the balance sheet, I gave this category a buy rating after considering the D/E, credit ratings, asset risk exposure, and CET1 ratio.

First, let's consider this firm's D/E ratio vs. peers, which is actually quite favorable to its risk profile. Its D/E of 1.10 not only has declined but also is now nearly the lowest in this particular peer group, painting this bank as one of lower leverage exposure.

Second, I do care about what major credit rating agencies are saying about STT. As of this writing, this bank has strong A-level ratings from the 3 major agencies:

Notable is what Fitch said in its note this March, writing that "STT's low-risk and highly liquid balance sheet is a key rating strength."

Now, on the asset side of the balance sheet, it's worth noting that STT is exposed to a considerable amount of investment securities and loans. Of this asset risk exposure, I think investors and analysts will especially pay attention to exposure to bad loans. The good news is that the trend in provisions for credit losses has been decreasing since 4Q24, a good sign (Q4 earnings release, pg. 7), but of course I think if this spikes again in FY26, we could see the stock getting impacted.

Overall, considering that State Street is listed as a global systemically important bank by the Financial Stability Board in 2025, it will especially be scrutinized due to the systemic risk of any major downturn at this bank. We can see that STT has continually maintained a strong CET1 ratio, for instance, well above regulatory minimums under Basel III standards:

So, while I know that was quite a lot to process, those are materially critical points to raise about such a systemically critical bank, and I think the evidence supports a bullish view in this section.

Dividend Outlook

Next, I considered dividend yield, growth, and coverage, determining this section deserves a buy rating.

The yield of around 2.7% is well below some of the high-yield stocks I've covered, particularly some mortgage REITs. (Example: Invesco Mortgage Capital has a yield of +18.6%).

However, this is why not all financial-related stocks should be thrown in the same basket, since State Street has a dividend payout ratio of only 31%, leaving lots of buffer in earnings. Earlier, I already showed that the firm has a strategy of investing a lot into technology/data, so I do not expect it to commit a huge portion of earnings to dividends.

I also considered sustainable long-term proven dividend growth through various cycles and shocks, and STT can boast a 10-year dividend CAGR of +9.2%, beating its sector average and showing resilience (pandemics, tariff wars, inflation, interest rate shocks).

Further, if the consensus EPS growth estimates materialize, this could further support the dividend in the near term as well as capex investment.

So, I am bullish on the dividend, despite the lower yield than some other stocks. After all, one cannot put in the same box a systemically critical bank and a mortgage REIT heavily impacted by interest rate risk. What wins for STT is its fees-driven diversification, a key reason I love covering asset managers.

Valuation Metrics

Based on my valuation worksheet below, which uses Seeking Alpha peer data, I gave this section a strong buy rating.

What we can learn from this worksheet is what I pointed out with checkmarks: that the stock is undervalued compared to this peer group average yet poses an upside forecast of +12%.

It is not too far from the average. Wall St. upside forecast of +17.7%, as of Monday morning.

While some stocks are undervalued for clear reasons and should trade cheap, this one seems like an undervaluation "opportunity" where the market is not paying a premium yet the firm has shown earnings growth, respectable margins, and organic growth (AUM net inflows, software bookings, etc.)

So, I am highly bullish in this case, betting that this is a classic undervaluation rather than a value trap.

Technical Charting

Now that we covered lots of fundamental analysis topics, in moving to technical analysis, I gave this stock a hold rating in this section.

While there are many types of technical charting to consider, for brevity I usually go with a 1-year price comparison vs. the 50-day/200-day simple moving average to spot any buy/sell/hold signals.

In the above chart I created, the last buy signal was last summer when the 50-day crossed above the 200-day SMA, a classic golden cross, and for a long time the 50-day acted as support to what seemed like bullish momentum.

More recently, though, and perhaps due to the overall market dip lately, that bullish case has broken, with the share price dipping finally below the short-term trend. However, the long-term trend is still pointing upward.

This appears to me more of a neutral pattern than a buy or hold, although some more optimistic readers may consider it a dip-buying opportunity on an otherwise fundamentally strong stock.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

A potential challenge to my bullish view, not covered elsewhere in this article, comes from the fact that although STT can be considered to have a sizable fee-driven business, as well as subscriptions to its Alpha platform, it still also has a considerable business tied to interest income. Income statement data shows, for instance, the year ending Dec. 2025 saw $2.9B in net interest income.

This can pose interest-rate risk for this bank, like many others. Mitigating some of this risk was the Fed's recent decision to keep its policy rates the same, which had already been predicted in CME FedWatch and which continues to predict rates staying the same after the upcoming late-April Fed meeting.

So, it mutes the potential of another rate shock, up or down, and this has already been baked into the share price by now.

Conclusion

For this anniversary coverage of STT, I ended up calling it a buy again and agreeing with the latest bullish view from Wall St. as of Monday morning.

This article showed that readers should consider this stock for their bank stocks watchlist as one that is a globally critical bank with signs of organic growth and resilience in the face of various market shocks/cycles but also has maintained A-level credit ratings and a compelling value proposition with its Alpha platform, making it more than just another custody bank or asset manager.

data.title: Willdan Group: Margin Strength And Secular Demand Drive Upside After Valuation Reset
data.description:
Willdan Group (WLDN) valuations have reset meaningfully toward historical highs seen pre-rally (levels seen last in 2023-24). The concerns that have led to this valuation reset are primarily a risk-off sentiment across the board in growth and high-valuation stocks and Willdan's own near-term growth normalization outlook.

The near-term growth normalization is off a high base, though, and is also driven by timing effects more than by any structural weakness. In fact, the secular tailwinds from electricity demand grid modernization, especially in an era of data center-driven demand, are very much intact. Late-cycle sentiments applicable to semis and other AI infrastructure plays are less applicable here as energy needs are a long lead item and look at long-term needs rather than cyclical troughs.

Overall, earnings growth prospects are still strong in my view, and this is additionally supported by earnings quality improvements (supported by margin expansion, operating leverage, and a mix shift toward higher-value energy services). Even if there is moderate multiple compression from here, the earnings growth should be able to generate decent returns for fresh entries.

Valuations Have Reset Meaningfully

The forward EV to EBITDA ratio has reset significantly from well above 20x levels to ~13.5x levels now. The prices still look elevated, as the 3-year chart below shows, but that has more to do with earnings catch-up than multiple expansion. The forward EV to EBITDA ratio was previously seen in the low double digits before the recent run-up in 2025 - around ~13x in late 2024 and ~11x in late 2023. So, from a valuation perspective, Willdan is no longer pricing in hypergrowth or any narrative excess - unless markets see late-cycle characteristics in the stock (which could push the ratio toward mid- to high-single-digits too, going by historical levels in the past 2-3 years).

I see fewer concerns around stagflation risks that have emerged lately for Willdan because its services and program management offerings have no direct commodity or construction costs exposure, so input cost spiraling concerns are relatively limited. The indirect effect of slowing growth in a stagflation scenario does mean risks of a slowdown, but unlike private capex, government budgets could be supportive, less cyclical, and not an immediate threat even in a slowing growth scenario. In fact, Willdan's demand remains secular due to energy security and data center themes - the business' longer development timelines ensure it smooths out private hyperscaler capex troughs and peaks. All in all, the valuation reset may have been driven by a market that is wary of higher valuations and a risk-off sentiment, but in the case of Willdan, the current forward EV to EBITDA levels are an opportunity for fresh entries from a valuation and broader fundamentals perspective (close to its own 5-year averages and only ~20% premium to the sector median).

Grid complexity is rising (transmission stress and intermittency are issues that need to be addressed). Energy efficiency as a theme is now a core requirement, with regulatory support to increase adoption. Commercial revenues have already grown to ~11% of total revenues and remain the fastest-growing area within Willdan (mostly supported by data center work). The overall high level of demand strength was highlighted by management in the Q4 2025 earnings call.

Electric load growth has returned to the United States after about 15 years of stagnation. Artificial intelligence and data centers are accelerating electricity demand at a scale not seen in recent years

Growth and Earnings Quality

The growth guidance for 2026 is a potential source of concern. At 7-11% net revenue growth (guided at $390-405m for the year), this is a step down from the ~16% CAGR growth seen over the past 4 years. The exact growth numbers are important to note, though, for each year in this period. Starting from 2022, the YoY growth has been 12%, 19%, 10%, and 23% chronologically - not a stable high-growth trajectory across the years. The stellar growth number was observed in 2025 when net revenue growth was remarkably higher, with around 17% growth in organic revenues.

The far slower implied growth for 2026 could be both a result of the higher base after a stellar year and conservative guidance. The latter cannot be ruled out. Willdan has a recent history of conservative guidance - the first guidance for 2025 was ~$325m for context, and actuals turned out ~12% higher. In either case, there are multiple reasons to feel comfortable around slowdown worries. First of all, management still maintains a 15-20% growth over time - that is both not lower than the current growth CAGR (last 4 years) and does not extrapolate hypergrowth narratives from the data center demand leg. The earnings call also revealed multi-year contracts (3-5 years) and a robust data center backlog extending into 2027-28. Some ramp is expected as the year progresses - for instance, management guides a Q2 ramp for LADWP.

Importantly, the growth thesis is bolstered by a growing EBITDA margin trajectory that supports earnings in the near term. As per data in the Q4 earnings deck, EBITDA margins have improved significantly from 2022 and seem to be sustaining well around 20% now. This is expected to hold up well as the energy segment (higher margins) accounts for 85% of revenue. Additionally, operating leverage remains supportive (as seen in the 2025 ~23% net revenue growth accompanied by a ~40% EBITDA growth). Management guidance itself sees EBITDA margins improving in 2026 at ~22%, highlighting a shift in mix and operating leverage even at slower than 2025 growth.

The Returns Math

While a stress low of a mid-single-digit EV to EBITDA ratio cannot be ruled out, the more rational floor in my view is around the ~10x mark (a little lower than the 2023-24 peaks). That alone means ~25% corrections from here on multiple compression alone. The adjusted EPS for the past 3 years has increased drastically (from ~$1.75 in 2023 to ~$4.89 in 2025). The guided 2026 EPS looks slightly lower at ~$4.6 at the midpoint. That is an accounting distortion, as there was a tax benefit boost in 2025 (179D). EBITDA growth is intact. The downside scenario therefore roughly translates to another 25% correction in my view. That still requires growth really stuck at 7-8%, margins plateauing, and the risk-off sentiment to persist.

A more realistic base case is that EPS grows by a conservative 10-12% CAGR over the next couple of years (far lower than the 2023-25 EPS growth trajectory), while the multiple holds. That alone should mean 20-25% returns. If growth reverts to the long-term 15-20% targets, given the secular tailwinds (interestingly not priced in looking at the valuations versus history), margins expand even slightly over the ~22% EBITDA margin expected in 2026, and EPS could compound more aggressively. That means more than the downside scenario we budgeted for, even if multiples do not expand meaningfully. If multiples expand as secular growth and data center support get factored in, returns could be even higher - which is where the asymmetry emerges from for a Buy call.

data.title: How Private Equity ETF BUYO May Help Manage AI Software Disruption Risk Vs. Illiquid Investments
data.description:
To start 2026, there has been ample coverage of the broad selloff in software stocks due to artificial intelligence ('AI') disruption risk. This selloff has not only rippled through public markets but also private equity and private credit.

This article will not explore the disruptive threat of AI to specific software products or other parts of the economy, which could be very real for some companies. Rather, it will build on our belief that the sell-off in publicly listed software and software-linked securities may have been broader than warranted.

During this process of figuring out the ultimate winners and losers in the software industry, it is important to keep in mind that indiscriminate selling almost always creates opportunities. Especially for active managers, and particularly for the nimblest active managers.

So, how best to harvest these opportunities?

Public Versus Private Investments During Market Turmoil

In rapidly evolving situations where winners and losers are being identified and priced accordingly in near real-time, should investors feel comfortable being locked into a set of illiquid private software companies that they can’t sell or trim exposure to in the near term?

Or might it be more prudent to have a liquid, private equity (PE)-mimicking portfolio that invests in publicly listed software companies and can be responsive to the dynamic situations surrounding individual companies and divest from specific holdings when appropriate? Then, as the dust settles and it becomes clearer which ones may face less disruption risk and thus may be mispriced, a strategy that can be nimble in picking its (re)entry points may allow investors to buy low and potentially capture the recovery more effectively than they can in illiquid private markets.

This is the dilemma currently facing many investors, as software is the most dominant industry-level concentration risk in private credit portfolios, at approximately 25% of exposure (possibly higher when different classifications are accounted for), and is a very meaningful exposure within private equity as well, at almost 15% of buyout funds.1

The thesis behind investing in Software as a Service ('SAAS') companies was sound at the time the majority of these investments were made (with some deals done during the infancy of or even predating OpenAI, Anthropic, and other popular AI firms), as these companies offered stable and predictable recurring revenue streams, what was thought to be a high cost of replacement, and other attractive features. And this may still be the case for many of these investments. Time will tell.

Meanwhile, public credit and equity indices have had far less direct exposure to the software and services industry than private markets in recent years. That said, there is still ample opportunity for more nimble active managers in public markets to divest from or avoid companies they deem to have a heightened risk of AI-driven disruption.

Along these lines, ‘liquid private equity’ strategies offer the potential to mimic many of PE’s leading return drivers while also featuring the responsiveness and agility to potentially avoid the individual software companies that may face headwinds or even existential threats from AI.

What Liquid Private Equity Strategies Currently Exist?

There are two leading families of liquid private equity strategies worth noting here, both viewed as proxies for PE return drivers but with very different approaches.

  1. One group seeks to mimic the return profile of PE funds by investing in companies with similar characteristics to PE holdings.
  2. The other group invests in publicly listed PE/private credit firms as well as Business Development Companies (BDCs) and other firms that are related to but perhaps on the periphery of PE.

It is the first group that we refer to above when saying that they can provide PE-like return drivers and exposures while remaining nimble in terms of selecting winners and losers within the software industry. We believe this is a more direct way to potentially generate PE-like returns.

The second group also presents an interesting case study during the recent software carnage, despite not being software companies. Shares of publicly listed PE/private credit firms have been selling off, as have many BDCs.

As noted, these two groups are the focus of the second set of exchange-traded funds (ETFs). While this is simply the market pricing in the heightened risk of the underlying assets in these firms’ portfolios and the potential impact on those funds' returns, it highlights the perils of using this narrower set of (largely financial services) securities as a proxy for PE portfolios and performance.

While some PE managers are narrow in their focus on software or broader technology investments, the overall PE asset class is more diversified in its industry exposures, and that diversification is a hallmark of a small cohort of liquid PE strategies that seek to mimic the actual key risks and return drivers of traditional PE, including its industry exposures.*

What to do now?

Does the lack of agility in responding to quickly evolving situations mean you should ignore private markets in wealth portfolios? Probably not, at least as the sole reason to ignore.

But does it mean that you should go in eyes wide open if you are adopting private markets and perhaps consider alternative ways to gain exposure to similar returns if you are not comfortable with some of the risks of traditional private exposure? In our opinion, absolutely!

This will potentially prove to be a Blockbuster moment for a number of software companies, but we believe it is highly improbable that it will be the beginning of the end for all software companies.

What the current software debate illuminates is an important question: when crises arise, how much of your money do you want to be stuck in a slow-moving, illiquid product with limited ability to sell the expected losers?

Private Equity ETF BUYO

For investors seeking exposure to the characteristics of companies targeted by PE, rather than direct ownership of specific private companies, there are now liquid strategies available.

These approaches use quantitative models to identify and mimic the exposures that have historically been observed in private equity investing. KraneShares partnered with Man Group, the world's largest publicly traded hedge fund manager and an alternative investments specialist2, to launch the KraneShares Man Buyout Beta Index ETF (BUYO). BUYO seeks to provide exposure to selected PE return drivers through a liquid, transparent ETF structure that is more cost-efficient than traditional PE’s standard headline 2&20 fee. This method can be thought of as a “smart beta” approach for PE, providing access to PE-like risk premia within a public market vehicle.

Just as ETFs exist to track broad asset classes like U.S. large-cap and small-cap equities, emerging markets, or various segments of fixed income, investors can now access selected PE return drivers through a liquid ETF structure.

Conclusion

As private market funds continue to be adopted quickly in wealth portfolios, we believe it is important to do so with your eyes wide open. As much as you or your clients may believe they don’t need liquidity, they don’t until they do. And if you reach that point where you need liquidity, you better be sure you actually have enough of it.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss. BUYO is non-diversified. The statement regarding diversification refers to sector exposure, not regulatory diversification under the Investment Company Act of 1940.

Citations:

  1. Data from "Software Stress & AI Risk in Private Credit," Prime Buchholz, as of 2/24/26.
  2. Data from "Managing the World’s Largest Publicly Traded Hedge Fund: Man Group CEO Robyn Grew," Goldman Sachs.

Index Definitions:

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500 Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that tracks the performance of 500 of the largest publicly traded companies in the United States.

Russell 2000 Index: The Russell 2000 Index is a market capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of approximately 2,000 small-cap publicly traded companies in the United States.

MSCI Emerging Markets Index: The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free float-adjusted, market-capitalization-weighted index that captures large- and mid-cap equities across emerging market countries.

MSCI World ex-US Index: The MSCI World ex USA Index is a free float-adjusted, market-capitalization-weighted index that captures large- and mid-cap equities across developed markets excluding the United States.

ICE BofA US Corporate Index: The ICE BofA US Corporate Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated, investment-grade corporate bonds publicly issued in the US domestic market.

ICE BofA US High Yield Index: The ICE BofA US High Yield Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated, below-investment-grade corporate bonds that are publicly issued in the US domestic market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CFTC CoTs: Managed Money Was Responsible For The Gold Sell-Off In January But Not March
data.description:
Please note: the CoTs report was published 03/27/2026 for the period ending 03/24/2026. “Managed Money” and “Hedge Funds” are used interchangeably.

The Commitments of Traders report is a weekly publication that shows the breakdown of ownership in the Futures market. For every contract, there is a long and a short, so the net positioning will always be zero, but the report shows who is positioned long or short. Historically, Hedge Funds (Managed Money) dominate the price action in both Gold and Silver.

Gold

Current Trends

Below shows net positioning for the 5 main groups of futures holders. Net positioning took a major step down at the end of January, right during the first big sell-off in gold. It is clear that this was the big driver of the move on the last trading day of January. This was speculative money getting washed out.

However, the move since the Iran war started was not speculative money. The open interest barely changed in the big price drop. It seems more likely this was actual selling, such as Turkey selling a vast amount of physical gold from reserves.

Figure 1: Net Position by Holder

Managed Money has been in complete control of the price action for years, driving the spikes in both directions. The overall upward trend is beyond the control of Managed Money but the short-term moves are very much tied to the action of Managed Money.

That changed dramatically in 2025 as the Managed Money group has been liquidating positions even as prices have continued rising. Managed Money may have been a driver of the initial sell-off in January, but they have been very quiet since. Again, this implies that the move from the Iran War has not been speculative traders.

Figure 2: Managed Money Net Position

Weekly Activity

In the latest week, there were some liquidations from Managed Money as the gold price finally broke $5,000, but it was very small by any historical standard. This move would not have been enough to bring the gold price from $5100 to $4400.

Figure 3

The activity in the options market remains relatively quiet despite all the recent volatility in gold. There has been a general uptick since 2024, but nothing material has changed since 2026 started.

Figure 4: Options Positions

Silver

Current Trends

Similar to gold, silver has seen general open interest decrease as the price increased. Some of this is related to higher margins making it more expensive to hold contracts. Regardless, Open Interest was falling as the price was going up and up.

Figure 5: Net Position by Holder

Unlike gold, which saw the steep drop in Managed Money right during the big price drop in January, silver was seeing speculative money steadily buy all during January. In fact, the week of the massive price drop in January only saw a few thousand contracts liquidated, which is really nothing in the big scheme. So, while the gold washout may have been driven by speculative money, the silver washout was not. It may have been that silver was just overstretched and then followed gold lower, but in a big way.

Another interesting point is seeing the managed money increasing their position each week following the January spike down. Thus, while silver prices have remained under pressure, Managed Money has been buying the entire time.

Figure 6: Managed Money Net Position

Weekly Activity

Despite the buying, you can see in the weekly chart below that this buying has been relatively modest by historical standards.

Figure 7: Net Change in Positioning

Unlike gold, the silver options market has seen much greater activity in recent months. This is exactly where the speculative trading could have been driving the market. The futures market is still significantly larger than the options market, but there is certainly a possibility that the action here was having an outsize move on the price.

Figure 8: Options Positions

Conclusion

The data is pretty clear here:

  • The gold washout in January was likely tied to or amplified by Managed Money
  • Silver was not being driven by Managed Money despite a much bigger price move
  • The drop in gold in March is not tied to Managed Money
  • Similar to gold, silver Managed Money cannot be linked to the move in March – in fact, they were buying

The table below shows the correlation of Managed Money and Other to the price moves. While Managed Money is usually highly correlated to the price action, this relationship reversed in 2025; prices rose as Managed Money liquidated. So far, in 2026, the correlation has dropped to zero for silver but remains negative for gold.

With Managed Money having very low exposure, it would seem there is a lot of money that could push up the price. Friday was a very strong day in metals: the stock market sold off, the DXY rose, and gold rose. This is the exact price action that shows gold and silver may have bottomed. The positioning of Managed Money along with the activity at the Comex further supports this case. This dip is a buying opportunity that might not last long!

Figure 9: Correlation Table

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fox Factory: A Turnaround Story That Requires Patience
data.description:
Background

Fox Factory (FOXF) is trading near $15.88, down 49% from its 52-week high of $31.18. The stock decline reflects real operational challenges as its margin compressed from 18.3% in FY2021 to 11.5% in FY2025. This was driven by revenue contraction, acquisition integration costs, and OEM disruptions. In February 2026, the company responded by bringing in activist investor Engine Capital and launching a two-phase profit optimization plan targeting $75 million in total savings. Phase 1 has already delivered $25 million in cost savings. Phase 2 is now in progress, and management has guided EBITDA margin recovery to 13.7% in FY2026. I am neutral on this turnaround story for now and will reassess the thesis after the company reports its Q1 2026 earnings.

The company makes high-performance parts for sports and off-road vehicles. It sells its products to manufacturers (OEMs) and directly to customers through three segments: Powered Vehicles, Aftermarket, and Specialty Sports.

Can Engine Capital Help Optimize Operations?

Fox Factory entered into a cooperation agreement with activist investor Engine Capital. The agreement resulted in two new independent directors joining the board and the formation of a Transformation Oversight Committee. The committee is focused on operational excellence and margin improvement. Engine Capital has a reputation for driving operational accountability in struggling companies. They typically focus on cost structure, profit optimization, and boardroom governance.

What does this mean for investors? The Transformation Oversight Committee will actively monitor margin improvement progress. As per the Q4 2025 press release, Phase 1 of the profit optimization plan has been executed successfully, delivering $25 million in cost savings. This helped the company offset some of the negative impact of tariffs.

CEO Mike Dennison acknowledged the progress: "This comprehensive effort, focused on footprint optimization and continuous improvement across all three operating segments, demonstrated our ability to grow revenue in challenging conditions while executing critical operational initiatives."

Further, the company has also initiated Phase 2 of the profit optimization strategy. The focus is on high-margin products and streamlining operations for efficiency. The target is $50 million in cost savings for FY2026. In my view, Phase 2 creates a more credible recovery story. However, turnarounds are complex and take time. Investors should watch the Q1 2026 earnings call closely for the first update on Phase 2 progress and any commentary from the new board of directors.

EBITDA Margin: The Question is Whether Margins Can Recover?

Data in $ million FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24 FY25 FY26 *
Revenue 1,299.1 1,602.5 1,464.2 1,393.9 1,467.3 1372.0
Growth 23.4% -8.6% -4.8% 5.3% -6.5%
EBITDA 237.8 292.2 228.9 167.0 168.4 188.5
Margin 18.3% 18.2% 15.6% 12.0% 11.5% 13.7%

Compiled by the author using data from Seeking Alpha. FY 2026* revenue and EBITDA are based on the midpoint of management guidance.

The margin compressed sharply from 18.3% in FY 2021 to 11.5% in FY 2025. This compression is the reason Engine Capital was brought in to control the situation and bring stability through the Transformation Oversight Committee. The key question for investors is simple. Is the cost structure fundamentally sustainable? The company is addressing this through two phases of profit optimization.

  • Phase 1 has already delivered $25 million in savings for FY 2025.
  • Phase 2 is in progress with a target of $50 million in savings for FY 2026.

As a result, management has guided FY 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $174 million–$203 million at a midpoint margin of 13.7%. This translates to ~200 basis points of EBITDA margin improvement on revenue guidance of $1,328 million–$1,416 million, implying negative revenue growth at the midpoint. In my view, this is the most important data point for investors in this thesis. If management can deliver ~200 bps of margin improvement on declining revenue, it proves the cost structure is sustainable. And when revenue starts recovering with improving market conditions, that will translate to even higher margins. Whether the company can return to its historical high of 18% margin is the question investors need to track as this turnaround story unfolds.

Balance Sheet: The Risk Investors Must Understand

Data in $million FY23 FY24 FY25
Cash 83.6 71.7 58.0
Total Debt 743.5 705.1 674.3
Short-Term Debt 14.3 24.3 26.9
Long-Term Debt 729.2 680.8 647.4
Net Debt 659.9 633.4 616.3
EBITDA 228.9 167.0 168.4
Net Debt/EBITDA 2.88x 3.79x 3.66x

Compiled by the author using data from Seeking Alpha.

In FY 2025, the company has $647.4 million in long-term debt and $26.9 million in short-term debt against just $58.0 million in cash. Net debt stands at $616.3 million with a net leverage ratio of 3.66x EBITDA. This is not extreme, but it is on the higher side given the ongoing revenue and margin headwinds.

What concerns me more is the trend. Over the last three years, total debt has been reduced by $69 million. But EBITDA has declined at a faster pace, resulting in a rising net leverage ratio. This is not a good indicator for the company. The company has high debt compared to EBITDA, and it limits management flexibility. It restricts expansion plans and puts pressure on loan covenants. I have not gone through the specific covenant details, but in general, any breach of covenant conditions significantly limits what management can do. Any further deterioration in revenue or a failure to deliver Phase 2 savings could pressure the company's ability to service its debt comfortably.

In a nutshell, investors should watch the net leverage ratio closely in every earnings report. The target should be below 2.5x as margin recovery progresses. Any movement in the wrong direction is a red flag and must be tracked carefully.

Final Thoughts

In my view, this is not a case of a broken business model or a broken cost structure. The issues at Fox Factory are cyclical in nature. But the challenges have been aggravated by acquisition integration costs and revenue contraction, which made a difficult situation worse. To address this, the company has brought in Engine Capital and launched a profit optimization plan for improving performance.

For now, I am neutral on this turnaround story. Investors need to patiently track the outcome of these initiatives. The key metrics to watch are EBITDA margin improvement, debt levels, and any further phases of profit optimization beyond Phase 2. In a nutshell, this is a wait-and-watch situation for me.

data.title: Bruker: A Better, Yet Speculative Proposition
data.description:
Shares of Bruker (BRKR) have come under some renewed pressure as of late, as shares are off to a soft start in 2026. In fact, shares are trading at levels around $33, having fallen some 40% from levels seen in the first week of trading this year! This came after investors were not impressed by the results for the final quarter of 2025 and the accompanied outlook for 2026.

That did not come completely unexpectedly to me, as I concluded towards the year-end of 2025 that Bruker did not provide a healthy diagnosis to investors. Currently, the setup has improved given a serious renewed setback in the share price, as well as some comforting guidance for 2026, with the two developments gradually creating a more compelling situation here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Muddling Through

In February, Bruker reported its 2025 results, with reported revenues for the year up 2% to $3.44 billion, as fourth-quarter revenues came in dead flat. In between some M&A efforts pursued during the year and favorable currency movements, the company ended the year with a minus 5% organic growth rate, which is hardly convincing. For the year, organic growth declines came in at minus 4%.

These are soft results, despite, in general, a good positioning, as well as solid diversification between the major geographic regions of the world and across segments. Non-GAAP earnings fell by fifty-eight cents to $1.83 per share, this number being quite adjusted with GAAP losses reported at $0.15 per share. Many of these adjustments relate to amortization, impairment, and restructuring charges, or at least not stock-based compensation expenses, but still the adjustments feel large.

Net debt was reported at $1.57 billion, a substantial number in relation to a $518 million adjusted operating income number reported for 2025, and while EBITDA could be a bit higher, leverage ratios are seen around 3 times.

In fact, leverage has caused quite an overhang, as 152 million shares trading at $33 grant equity a $5.0 billion equity valuation. This results in a $6.6 billion enterprise valuation, valuing the business at around 2 times sales, and if we trust the adjusted earnings metrics, shares trade at 18 times adjusted earnings.

Despite the soft performance, the company guided for growth to return in 2026. Reported sales are seen up 4-5% to $3.57-$3.60 billion, with organic sales growth seen between 1-2%. This should result in earnings improving to $2.10-$2.15 per share, despite anticipated currency headwinds.

And Now?

In between a return of growth in 2026 and a setback in the share price, valuations have come down a bit. Based on the revised guidance, earnings multiples are seen coming down to 15-16 times, as incremental movements will help in reducing leverage a bit as well. This makes it so that valuations have come in quite a bit since December, when, frankly, I was a bit surprised by the rally seen in the share price from the lows.

Moreover, outside the modest growth guidance for 2026, there was more good news. At the start of the year, Bruker's BEST division reported a combined $500 million order value from two larger customers, with radiology customers building the next generation of MRI magnets. Of course, this looks like a massive order for the business to contribute substantially in the coming years.

The company actually announced a bolt-on deal at the start of the year, acquiring another 60% ownership stake in Swiss-based TOFWERK AG to bring its ownership stake to a full 100%. The mass spectrometer business employs some 130 workers, generating some $40 million in annual sales with no other financial terms reported here.

Struggling Despite Its Positioning

Bruker has a rich corporate history, with Bruker founded in 1960 to provide a wide range of scientific instruments to high-end customers in life sciences, but also semiconductors, materials, and packaging. Despite positioning itself in growth markets and catering to high-end customers with sophisticated technologies, Bruker's shares have been lagging a lot, now down to levels first seen around 2018 (absent of a short boom during the dot-com bubble).

Quite some has happened meanwhile, with shares nearly hitting the $100 mark in 2021, driven by Bruker's role during the pandemic, as shares traded near similar levels early in 2024. Solid organic growth was reported at the time, as well as enthusiasm driving shares, but something strange happened. Bruker went on a massive acquisition spree early in 2024, with some acquisitions being small, but others running in the hundreds of millions. The cash flow statement for that year revealed over $1.5 billion in M&A efforts running through the statement, as the size, but, moreover, the number of acquisitions pursued in a short period of time was surprising.

As organic growth slowed and debt was added, the company actually felt the need to issue equity around $60 late in the spring that year, even after shares were down a third from levels seen just months before. That was bad enough as it is, as the company originally guided for solid sales growth in 2025, with adjusted earnings seen at $2.70 per share. The earnings performance eventually came in nearly a dollar per share lower.

That did not withhold the business from making some bolt-on acquisitions in 2025, albeit the pace of acquisitions slowed down dramatically, but as reported, dealmaking continued early in 2026. Slower growth actually triggered Bruker to raise $600 million on convertible preferred stock in September of last year, which, if included, makes that leverage a bit higher.

Turning More Upbeat

Between the pullback since the end of 2025 and some stability in the sense that modest sales and earnings growth are seen in 2026, I am gradually turning a bit more upbeat here. This comes as the risk-reward proposition has changed on both sides. Given a 15-16 times adjusted earnings multiple, high leverage, and soft track record, the situation is not necessarily very compelling, yet I am gradually warming up to Bruker again.

While it is very difficult to build conviction given the uneven track record, I am gradually warming up again to the shares here. This makes me willing to consider a small speculative position on slightly further dips. However, I fail to have the conviction to build a position in size, given the uneven past track record, including that of capital allocation decisions, which continue to amaze me.

data.title: Axon: Don't Pull The Trigger Yet (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As shown below, it has been nearly a year now since I initiated Axon Enterprise, Inc. (AXON) with a sell rating. While that article was primarily focused on the technical side of things, I found 2025 guidance to be disappointing and the stock to be grossly overvalued. Even though Axon advanced after that article was published, it has now given up all those gains and then some. As of this writing, the stock is down more than 20% over this period while the S&P500 is up over 20%. That is massive underperformance.

With the company having reported earnings just in late February, today I'll be providing a much needed update to see whether the investment thesis has changed.

In the below analysis, it is shown that while the long-term opportunity is large and the Q4 results are overall solid, growth guidance for 2026 may be a little disappointing. Furthermore, the company is facing some risks, such as ICE involvement, and so despite the significant contraction in the valuation, I believe the risk/reward setup for investors isn't attractive enough yet. While I've decided to upgrade Axon, I believe investors should hold off on pulling the trigger for now.

The Opportunity at Hand

Before we look at the numbers specific to their 2025 Q4, let's discuss how big the long-term opportunity is for Axon. As shown in the infographic above, their TAM has now reached $159 billion, and in my view that suggests that the runway for growth is robust. Investors should note that the company saw $2.78 billion worth of revenues for 2025 as a whole, so if they are able to execute and capitalize on the size of this market, prospects are bright. As stated in the infographic, U.S. State & Local TAM is less than 15% penetrated, so the opportunity seems large as the world shifts to the next generation of enforcement equipment. In their investor deck, they highlighted the uptrend in U.S. Public Safety spending, so the market itself is set to continue expanding.

Another thing I wanted to point out is that Axon is, in fact, quite diversified in its offerings. With the company formerly called Taser International, it isn't hard to see why investors mostly associate them with that product. However, as shown in the above infographic, Taser & Training represents just $14 billion of their TAM. On the hardware side of things, Axon now offers advanced solutions like drones and robotics, while on the software side of things they offer services like digital evidence management as well as AI tools. Therefore, investors should recognize that the company is in fact involved with potentially higher-growth opportunities than just their Taser products.

Growth at Multiyear Highs

Let's now get into the results for 2025 Q4. In the quarter, Axon generated revenues of $797 million, representing a growth rate of 39% YoY. They stated that this was "driven by premium software adoption, TASER 10, Axon Body 4 and counter-drone equipment," so they are indeed more than just a taser company. As shown in the chart above, growth has accelerated rather steeply to a multiyear high level. That suggests that business activity is strong and that they seem to be capturing the opportunity that's present in a nice manner. Furthermore, Axon beat analyst expectations by $41.43 million, so the fourth quarter was one that showed clear outperformance. Based on the top line, the company is executing nicely.

Demand is Strong

While revenue growth already tells quite a bit about where demand is currently, the four metrics above show that the strong top-line growth we saw in Q4 could potentially be sustained. In terms of bookings and ARR, you can see that there was a considerable jump for both metrics YoY as a sign that future business activity will be strong. The fact that nearly all revenues are from customers on subscription plans makes their revenues durable. Lastly, perhaps most importantly, Axon's net revenue retention has continued to improve YoY. This is a key metric as it shows that customers are liking the user experience. Axon, therefore, has a solid value proposition, and of course that is an encouraging sign.

Margins & Profitability

On the right, you can see that in annual terms, Axon's adjusted EBITDA margin is clearly moving in the right direction. Therefore, in the grand scheme of things, profitability seems to be improving. For Q4 specifically, there are some slightly mixed numbers. In the fourth quarter, adjusted gross margin contracted by 210 basis points to 61.1% as the company cited tariffs and product mix. Even for a law enforcement equipment company, tariffs have eroded cost control and dented profitability. While this isn't good news, do note that they still managed to report an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.9% for Q4. That is a considerable improvement from the year-ago quarter's 24.6%, so Axon has made up for that gross margin weakness with nice corporate efficiency, it seems.

Outlook: 2026 & Beyond

Axon provided some guidance for full-year 2026. For revenue, the range shown above would be a deceleration from what we saw in 2025. That is quite disappointing given the large TAM that was outlined earlier and the strong demand metrics we went over. With bookings having grown by 43% YoY, I would have expected stronger top line projections. Therefore, execution relative to the opportunity at hand may be less than perfect. For adjusted EBITDA margin, things are projected to stay unchanged, so despite the tariff-related gross margin pressure, Axon's core profitability is set to remain resilient moving forward.

In addition to these 2026 estimates, the company also gave targets for 2028. By then, Axon sees revenues of around $6 billion. If you pull out your calculator, you can see that their target potentially hints at continued growth in the high twenties percentage range for 2027 and 2028. Therefore, while the slowdown from 2025 isn't desirable, the company is still in a phase of sustained, resilient growth. For adjusted EBITDA margin, they are targeting 28% for 2028, so moving forward, the company expects to continue improving efficiency and profitability.

Risks

One of the key risks for Axon is its involvement with ICE in the United States. While the company's moonshot goal is to reduce gun-related deaths between the police and the public, the use of their equipment and solutions for a highly controversial mission sets the company up for reputational risk.

In mid-March, Axios Denver reported that Axon is the company that the mayor's administration has chosen to take over their automated license plate reader system. The only problem is that Axon's strong ties with the Department of Homeland Security mean that the company has a relationship with ICE. In Denver, there has reportedly been public denouncing of ICE tactics and policies, so it is clear why there has been some controversy. In my view, this is a clear example of the reputational risk Axon is facing under this Trump Administration.

Valuation Much More Reasonable

As shown in the chart above, Axon's forward P/E ratio has contracted by a massive amount since mid last year. Readings of over 120 weren't unheard of, so at the current level of 55.27, the valuation seems to be at much more reasonable levels now. I would say this may help to justify a revision to a hold rating.

In today's analysis, it was shown that the long-term opportunity is sizeable as the TAM makes it clear that the company has room to grow. Q4 results were encouraging as top-line growth accelerated to multiyear highs and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded. However, while demand metrics were robust, 2026 guidance was shown to be a little disappointing. The margin target for 2028 is quite encouraging, but growth may remain in just the high 20s percentage range. I expected better exploitation of the large market opportunity moving forward, and with risks like tariff cost pressures and ICE involvement present, I would say that the contraction in the valuation hasn't been completely unjustified.

At the end of the day, a forward P/E of over 50 is not particularly cheap for a company that's projecting top-line growth in the high twenties for 2026. That said, given the long-term potential of the business and the huge correction in the valuation, I do believe the risk/reward is now more or less neutral.

Conclusion

Nearly a year ago, I initiated Axon at a sell rating, and ultimately my bearish thesis has played out quite well. Today, I have decided that it's time to upgrade the stock to a hold as the fundamental setup is around neutral. A large long-term opportunity and strong Q4 results are met with mixed guidance and some notable risks. The valuation has come down but still isn't particularly cheap, so the risk/reward seems quite balanced currently. Therefore, I believe investors should hold off on pulling the trigger at this point despite the fact that the stock is more attractive now than it has been.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/31/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: EVT: Time For Active Management For Equity Positioning
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with diversified equity exposure with the goal of generating high distributable income. Managed equity strategies may be exceptionally appealing during periods of significant market disruption, particularly as major indices face exogenous pressure, making successful stock selection a highly valued skill.

With mounting uncertainty regarding the global supply chain disruptions raised from the Iranian war, I believe investors should take into consideration managed CEFs like EVT as a component of a diversified portfolio strategy. Given the near-term risks in the market and comparable historical performance, I am recommending EVT with a Hold rating.

Investment Thesis

EVT was designed to invest in value-oriented, dividend-paying stocks that may provide tax-advantaged dividend income as part of its investment objective, presenting an appealing opportunity given the state of the broader equity market. Prior to the Iranian war, I believe the markets were heavily shifting in favor of value over growth stocks as investors began questioning the quality of the AI theme and whether the high premiums appropriately priced the stocks, as well as the software stock disruption with the emergence of the theme that AI may replace traditional enterprise software.

While I don’t believe this second factor will necessarily be meaningfully impactful to the market given how integrated enterprise software has become, tethered together with custom applications, I do expect AI to disrupt the market with respect to being more of a companion feature. Granted, this is highly selective, as a CRM or ERP company will be less likely disrupted when compared to a creative suite.

I believe the recent events in the Middle East added fuel to the flame, adding pressure to oil prices and uncertainty towards global supply chains, as a large proportion of the world’s oil and natural gas comes out of the Middle East through the Strait of Hormuz. With LNG capacity coming offline resulting from missile strikes, the global gas supply may be disrupted for years to come. The economic impact was higher oil and natural gas prices.

Overall, I believe this is one of the leading factors influencing the equity markets, adding pressure and significant uncertainty.

With the global market being disrupted, I believe there is no greater time than now to be a stock picker or offload the risk to professional asset managers that can actively manage their equity strategy through diversification. One of the values EVT provides is that the fund provides value and dividend exposure as well as broad sector diversification, enabling the portfolio management team to dynamically position the portfolio to mitigate risks related to the war in the Middle East.

The risk at hand is that EVT may be exposed to a challenging market environment. The top holding within the strategy is Micron (MU) at 3.96%. While I have reason to believe that Micron is well positioned for growth given the successful sales of high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerator chips, the company may face certain headwinds relating to the war as the global supply chain for helium is being disrupted, which may impact the rate of chip production in the coming quarters. Accordingly, Qatar supplies a third of the world’s helium, a major bottleneck given that the Ras Laffan facility was struck by missiles and experienced extensive damage.

Helium is used during the etching process when fabricating semiconductors, ensuring that the silicon doesn’t overheat and crack.

This challenge could significantly impact companies whose growth is underpinned on sourcing AI accelerators to expand their data center scale, including Amazon (AMZN), which is held at 2.96%, Alphabet (GOOG) at 2.54%, and to a lesser degree, Meta Platforms (META) at 1.76%.

Aside from the sector risk involved, I believe the value- and dividend-oriented investment strategy can provide investors with an appealing proposition as a way to seek out investment durability over high growth potential. While I expect value and dividend stocks to experience similar negative pricing action as a result of the market sell-off, I do not believe that the sell-off will be as extensive when compared to growth-oriented stocks that may trade at higher premiums, providing investors with some cushion to soften the blow.

About Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

EVT was launched on September 30, 2003 by Eaton Vance. EVT has a total expense ratio of 227bps, 103bps of which are management fees. EVT presently has $2.1b in net assets, inclusive of 18.1% leverage applied to the share assets. Leverage can be appealing for income-focused strategies as it may provide investors with amplified exposure to distributable income, though it may also increase risk exposure.

EVT is diversified across 75 holdings, 79.30% of which are equities and 19.43% fixed income & preferred stock. The strategy is primarily allocated to US equities at 92.99%, with some exposure in Europe and Latin America. The strategy is most heavily weighted in the financial sector, making up 31.77% of the portfolio weight. Other sector concentrations include industrials at 12.18%, health care at 9.22%, and information technology at 9.13%.

EVT pays out a monthly distribution with a forward rate of $1.98/share, yielding 8.31%. EVT’s year-to-date distributions have primarily derived from net realized long-term capital gains [71.5%] and net investment income [16.2%], suggesting that the distribution was largely influenced by stock selection and rotation and, to a lesser degree, dividend income.

Comparing performance, EVT has historically performed in the middle of the pack, with the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) being the top performer over most historical holding periods. ADX also charges a relatively lower fee to investors with an expense ratio of 56bps, far below peer strategies.

ADX also presents an appealing distribution at 8.68%, just above EVT’s distribution rate of 8.31%. Given the historical performance, fees, and robust distribution, ADX may be considered as an alternative investment strategy for those seeking equity income and growth.

Risks Related to EVT

EVT is an actively managed, income-oriented investment strategy designed to maximize total returns for investors by investing in value- and dividend-paying stocks, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Investors in EVT may be exposed to manager risk and the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight securities. With emerging events in the Middle East and the major disruption to the global supply chain, I believe the portfolio management team will be especially tested as to their ability to mitigate risk relating to global trade. US equities may continue to face pressure from high oil prices and the continuation of the Iranian war, potentially negatively impacting equity strategies.

Final Thoughts

The equity market may face significant pressure resulting from the Iranian war and the disruption to the global supply chain, making security selection a crucial skill in creating a successful portfolio strategy. I believe the thematic approach to value and dividend security selection can be appealing for investors as software and AI are rotated out of favor, though EVT presents significant exposure to the AI theme through its investments in the hyperscalers and chip producers. When comparing historical performance, the low-cost CEF ADX has outperformed over most historical periods, presenting an appealing alternative for those seeking dividend income.

Given these factors, I am recommending EVT with a Hold rating.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Structural Tailwinds Make It A Golden Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

I haven’t had the chance to review Barrick Mining Corporation (B) yet, while covering peers in the mining industry that tend to have a broader asset base. I will now take the plunge following the rally in gold prices and the ensuing normalization despite macro risks ticking up.

There are a lot of inherent catalysts to the firm’s valuation, such as gold prices, record FCF and the potential incoming IPO of Barrick’s NA gold assets. I will go through them to see if B will continue to offer strong value to shareholders.

Current Dynamics

I’ll start with the macro backdrop that has pushed gold to rally and falter with the Iran situation. Gold started the year at around $4,384 per ounce and pushed forward to nearly $5,500 an ounce. It now came back to $4,532 as of the last available prices. The current price drivers are structural in my mind, rather than simply speculative, as central bank demand is roughly 585 tonnes per quarter through 2026, as sovereign wealth funds are diversifying away from dollar-denominated assets. More so, the US-China trade tensions have pushed the Chinese government to diversify away from US treasuries toward gold. The PBOC has been a main actor in this, with its 15th consecutive month of reserve increases bringing the total official gold holdings to 2,308 tonnes. Regulation also played in this facet as Chinese institutional investors are now allowed to allocate up to 1% of their assets directly into bullion.

Furthermore, many believe that gold should have continued its rise in price, considering the increased macro risks linked to the Iran conflict, but prices have fallen sharply and have been highly volatile ever since. The downward pressure is essentially linked to the rise in crude prices due to the Strait of Hormuz, which is fueling global inflation fears. This would thus translate in a higher interest rate environment or even increase rates to combat the shock, pushing treasury yields higher, thus increasing the demand for US treasuries, reducing the demand for non-yielding assets like gold. The systemic shock of a war also pushed investors to sell assets in order to go back to cash and wait it out.

On Barrick Gold’s side, the conflict has had a direct impact on the Reko Diq copper-gold project in Pakistan, which has seen reduced activity and spending.

Barrick has also decided to spin off its NA assets into a single entity, which is a drastic pivot from the conglomerate model that has kept share prices down compared to pure geographical plays like Agnico Eagle. This will basically reduce the geopolitical risks of international operations in countries like Mali and Papua New Guinea. By doing so, B is hoping that this will force a sum-of-the-parts re-rating recognizing the inherent value of the Tier-1 assets.

The new company will be a concentration of the world’s highest-quality gold assets, with the centerpiece being the participation in the Nevada Gold Mines, which is the world’s largest gold mining joint venture. NGM has seen a strong performance in 2025 thanks to its operational synergies that have led to a 25% sequential increase in production from the Carlin complex. NewCo will also own the Formile project, which has been dubbed the gold discovery of the century and acts as the main organic growth engine for the new company. Recent drilling has doubled the inferred resources to nearly 8MM ounces at world-leading grades. NewCo is also expected to be a high-yield cash machine.

The IPO will be a float of 10 to 15% of the total shares while retaining a 90% ownership stake, though the valuation is currently threatened by a legal firestorm with Newmont (NEM).

Valuation

Despite the rise in gold prices, the company is relatively cheap, even the cheapest among its peers, with its P/E GAAP of 13.16, below Newmont’s 15.98 and Agnico Eagle Mines' (AEM) at 21.83. Though despite the high profitability levels, the firm has seen its FCF yield hang at a relatively medium level at 3.3%. The company also has a slim dividend yield of 2.19%, that has grown tremendously well following the rise in gold prices, with a 5YR CAGR of 20.69%. I also wanted to dive deeper outside of simple financial sand, that’s why I did a sum of the parts analysis and a scenario analysis, taking into account the IPO of NewCo.

As you can see, the SOTP model would imply a fair value of $43.02 which would only be an upside of 11.5%, despite the IPO of the NewCo. But the bull case mentioned in the scenario analysis would potentially give a 13x premium IPO multiple, thus making the fair value of legacy Barrick’s mining share surge to $50.18 per share, which I see as highly possible if this environment persists.

Growth-wise, I’m bullish on Copper, which has been deemed a multi-decade strategic vector by the company. Management has projected that copper will amount to nearly 40% of the company’s EBITDA by 2030, thanks to the global energy transition tailwind as well as AI-related infrastructure. B has guided for a production of 190,000 to 220,000 tonnes of copper with an AISC of between $3.45 and $3.75 per pound. The company has also restored a normal situation in Mali with the Loulo-Gounkoto development, which will produce around 260K to 290K ounces for 2026, which is a drastic reintegration of production.

Risks

The biggest risk would be the current legal firestorm with Newmont, which is the junior partner in the NGM JV, alleging that there is a case of systematic mismanagement and resource piracy as Barrick allegedly diverted resources to its Fourmile project. Newmont is planning to block the NewCo IPO and it could further escalate in a possible buyout, dismantling the cornerstone of the NewCo valuation.

Barrick’s overall outlook has been digested as a transition year rather than straight growth, which has shocked some investors. B will produce less gold this year as it has guided at 2.9 to 3.25MM ounces due to maintenance and sequencing, though this would mean that Barrick will not fully capitalize on the highest gold prices in history.

Conclusion

If you want exposure to gold, I would always recommend buying a mining company in the field rather than owning the asset outright, considering the working asset aspect of holding shares. I believe that the recent drawback in stock offers investors a strong opportunity to get in, banking on a ton of structural tailwinds for the gold industry as a whole, and mainly Barrick Mining. I rate the company a Buy.
